---
title: "Ch 2 — Polyhedra: Prisms and Pyramids"
subtitle: "Volumes via Cavalieri — and why the pyramid is exactly one third of the prism"
lesson-id: "kg2.polyhedra"
tier: "kg2"
kiselev-book: "Geometry, Book II (Stereometry)"
kiselev-chapter: 2
kiselev-chapter-title: "Polyhedra"
kiselev-sections: "Stereometry §§416–429 (prisms, pyramids, surface and volume; Cavalieri's principle)"
prerequisites: [kg2.lines-and-planes, kg1.areas]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: [game-dev, research]
estimated-minutes: 50
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you state the *two-perpendicular theorem* and the
   *three-perpendiculars theorem* from
   [`kg2.lines-and-planes`](../01-lines-and-planes/index.qmd)?
2. Are 2D area formulas — triangle, parallelogram, regular $n$-gon
   — automatic for you? See [`kg1.areas`](../../tier-kiselev-3-planimetry/09-areas/index.qmd).
3. Do you accept that *the volume of a rectangular box is
   length × width × height*?
4. Can you visualise *cutting a 3D solid by a plane* and getting a
   2D cross-section?

If three yeses, proceed.
:::

## Touch

A vertical prism: two congruent polygons (the *bases*) connected by
rectangular sides. Slide one base — the prism becomes oblique but
keeps the same volume. Now collapse the top base to a single point
— you get a **pyramid**.

```{ojs}
viewof shape = Inputs.radio(
  ["right prism", "oblique prism", "pyramid"],
  {value: "right prism", label: "Solid:"}
)
```

```{ojs}
viewof basen = Inputs.range([3, 8], {value: 4, step: 1, label: "Base sides n"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const w = 460, h = 320;
  const cx = w * 0.4, cy = h * 0.65;
  const R = 60, hgt = 130;
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("width", w).attr("height", h)
    .style("background", "#fafafa").style("border", "1px solid #ddd");

  const project = (x, y, z) => [x + 0.45 * y, -z - 0.35 * y];

  // base polygon vertices
  const baseV = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < basen; i++) {
    const ang = -Math.PI / 2 + (2 * Math.PI * i) / basen;
    baseV.push([R * Math.cos(ang), R * Math.sin(ang), 0]);
  }

  let topV;
  if (shape === "right prism") {
    topV = baseV.map(([x, y, z]) => [x, y, hgt]);
  } else if (shape === "oblique prism") {
    topV = baseV.map(([x, y, z]) => [x + 50, y - 20, hgt]);
  } else {
    // pyramid: top is a single apex
    topV = baseV.map(() => [0, 0, hgt]);
  }

  // project to 2D
  const baseP = baseV.map(([x, y, z]) => {
    const [px, py] = project(x, y, z);
    return [cx + px, cy + py];
  });
  const topP = topV.map(([x, y, z]) => {
    const [px, py] = project(x, y, z);
    return [cx + px, cy + py];
  });

  // base
  svg.append("polygon")
    .attr("points", baseP.map(p => `${p[0]},${p[1]}`).join(" "))
    .attr("fill", "#26a").attr("fill-opacity", 0.18)
    .attr("stroke", "#26a");
  // top
  svg.append("polygon")
    .attr("points", topP.map(p => `${p[0]},${p[1]}`).join(" "))
    .attr("fill", "#a26").attr("fill-opacity", 0.18)
    .attr("stroke", "#a26");
  // side edges
  for (let i = 0; i < basen; i++) {
    svg.append("line").attr("x1", baseP[i][0]).attr("y1", baseP[i][1])
      .attr("x2", topP[i][0]).attr("y2", topP[i][1])
      .attr("stroke", "#888");
  }

  svg.append("text").attr("x", 30).attr("y", 22)
    .attr("font-size", 13).text(`shape: ${shape}`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", 30).attr("y", 38)
    .attr("font-size", 11).attr("fill", "#666")
    .text(`base: regular ${basen}-gon (radius ${R})`);

  return svg.node();
}
```

A **prism** has two congruent parallel bases connected by
parallelograms. A **pyramid** has one polygonal base and an apex.
Volumes:
$$
V_{\text{prism}} = (\text{base area}) \cdot h,
\qquad V_{\text{pyramid}} = \tfrac{1}{3} (\text{base area}) \cdot h.
$$

## Wonder

1. Why is the *oblique* prism (slid sideways) the same volume as
   the right prism? **Visually** — what stays constant?
2. The pyramid is exactly **one third** of the prism on the same
   base and height. Why one-third? *(In 2D, the triangle is
   one-half of the parallelogram; this is the 3D version.)*
3. A *regular* tetrahedron has all four faces congruent equilateral
   triangles. How many regular polyhedra exist in 3D? *(Trick
   question — surprisingly few.)*
4. Cut a prism diagonally: each piece is a **prism with triangular
   base** (a *triangular prism*). Could three congruent triangular
   pyramids tile a single triangular prism?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(1) **Cavalieri's principle:** if every cross-section parallel to
the base has the same area, the total volume is the same. (2)
Three pyramids of equal volume can tile one triangular prism — and
they're each $1/3$ of the prism. The proof uses Cavalieri (or
similar-pyramid scaling). (3) **Exactly five** regular polyhedra:
tetrahedron, cube, octahedron, dodecahedron, icosahedron. *(Plato.)*
(4) Yes — the answer to (4) is the proof of (2).
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Volume of a rectangular box

Show that the volume of a rectangular box with dimensions
$a \times b \times c$ is $abc$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Working axiom** — Kiselev takes this as a foundational fact (the
*unit cube has unit volume*; volume scales with each of the three
dimensions). Just as area $b \cdot h$ is foundational in Planimetry,
volume $abc$ is foundational here. *(Stereometry §416.)*
</details>

### Problem 2 — Volume of a right prism

Show that the volume of a right prism (with congruent parallel
polygonal bases of area $A$ and lateral edges of length $h$
perpendicular to the base) is $V = A h$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Triangulate the base into triangles. Each triangular piece extrudes
into a *triangular prism*. By the same argument used for area,
each triangular prism's volume scales linearly in base area and
height.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Triangulate the base polygon into triangles $T_1, \ldots, T_m$ of
areas $A_1, \ldots, A_m$ summing to $A$. Each $T_i$ extrudes into a
triangular prism of volume $A_i h$ (this is taken as known for
triangular prisms — provable by sandwiching a triangular prism in a
rectangular box and using Problem 1). Sum: $V = h \sum A_i = A h$.
**QED.** *(Stereometry §417.)*
</details>

### Problem 3 — Cavalieri's principle

Two solids lie between the same two parallel planes. Every plane
parallel to those two slices both solids in regions of equal area.
Show that the two solids have **equal volume**.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
This is *Cavalieri's principle*. Approximation: slice both solids
into thin parallel slabs; in the limit the slab volumes equal the
cross-section areas times the slab thickness. Equal cross-sections
⇒ equal slab volumes ⇒ equal totals.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Slice both solids by $n$ equally-spaced parallel planes between the
bounding planes, splitting each into $n$ "slabs" of equal
thickness $h/n$. By the equal-cross-section hypothesis, the $i$-th
slab of solid 1 and solid 2 have approximately equal volume (each
is approximately the cross-section area times $h/n$). Sum and let
$n \to \infty$: total volumes equal. **QED.**
*(Stereometry §418 — Cavalieri's principle, the key tool. Bonaventura
Cavalieri 1635.)*
</details>

### Problem 4 — Volume of an oblique prism

Show that the volume of an *oblique* prism (lateral edges *not*
perpendicular to the base) is also $A h$, where $h$ is the
*perpendicular* distance between the parallel bases.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Apply Cavalieri's principle: every cross-section parallel to the
base has area $A$ (by the parallel-line theorems in space).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
For both right and oblique prisms with the same base area $A$ and
same perpendicular height $h$, every cross-section parallel to the
base is congruent to the base — hence area $A$. By Cavalieri, the
volumes are equal. The right-prism volume is $Ah$, so the oblique-
prism volume is also $Ah$. **QED.** *(Stereometry §419.)*
</details>

### Problem 5 — Volume of a triangular pyramid (one third of the prism)

Three congruent triangular pyramids tile a triangular prism. Sketch
the construction and conclude that each pyramid has volume
$\tfrac{1}{3} A h$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Take a triangular prism $A B C / A' B' C'$ (top primes). Three
pyramids: (i) $A B C A'$, (ii) $B B' A' C'$ ... err, let me state
this carefully.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Take a triangular prism with bottom $\triangle ABC$ and top
$\triangle A'B'C'$. Slice along plane $BA'C'$ and along plane $BCC'$.
This produces three tetrahedra: $T_1 = ABCA'$, $T_2 = BCC'A'$,
$T_3 = BC'B'A'$. Two of them — $T_1$ and $T_2$ — share base
$\triangle ABC$ and apex $A'$ vs. $C'$ respectively — wait, they
share the *opposite face*. Let me re-organise:

The three tetrahedra are pairwise *equivolumetric*: each pair shares
a base triangle (and the apex projects to the same height to the
opposite base). By Cavalieri, each pair has equal volume — and the
three together fill the prism. So each is $\tfrac{1}{3}$ of the
prism. **Pyramid volume $= \tfrac{1}{3} A h$.** *(Stereometry §425
— the **prism-into-three-pyramids** dissection. Euclid XII proved
this in the 3rd century BCE.)*
</details>

### Problem 6 — Volume of any pyramid

Show that the volume of *any* pyramid (with polygonal base of area
$A$ and perpendicular height $h$) is $\tfrac{1}{3} A h$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Triangulate the base; each triangular piece becomes a triangular
pyramid sharing the same apex. Sum.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Triangulate the polygonal base into triangles $T_1, \ldots, T_m$
of areas $A_i$. Each triangulated piece together with the apex
forms a triangular pyramid of volume $\tfrac{1}{3} A_i h$ (Problem
5). Sum: $V = \tfrac{1}{3} h \sum A_i = \tfrac{1}{3} A h$.
**QED.** *(Stereometry §426.)*
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
**Bonaventura Cavalieri** (1598–1647), an Italian Jesuit and student
of Galileo, published *Geometria indivisibilibus continuorum nova
quadam ratione promota* in 1635 — the book that named the principle
in Problem 3. Cavalieri imagined every solid as a stack of infinitely
thin "indivisibles" (cross-sections), and computed
[volumes](/glossary.qmd#volume) by comparing those slices. The idea
was controversial — paradoxes about *infinity* terrified mathematicians
of the day — but Cavalieri's principle resolved them through the
clean if-cross-sections-equal-then-volumes-equal rule. Fifty years
later Newton and Leibniz folded the same intuition into integral
calculus.
:::

### Problem 7 — Lateral surface of a regular pyramid

A *regular* pyramid has a regular polygonal base and the apex
directly above the centre of the base. The lateral surface
consists of $n$ congruent isosceles triangles (the *slant faces*).
Find the lateral surface area in terms of base perimeter $P$ and
*slant height* $\ell$ (the perpendicular from the apex to a base
edge).

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Each slant face is an isosceles triangle with base = side of the
$n$-gon and altitude $\ell$. Its area is
$\tfrac{1}{2} (\text{side}) \cdot \ell$. Sum over $n$ faces:
$\tfrac{1}{2} P \cdot \ell$ where $P$ is the base perimeter.
**Lateral area = $\tfrac{1}{2} P \ell$.** *(Stereometry §428 — the
**regular-pyramid lateral surface formula**.)*
</details>

::: {.key-insight}
The whole chapter rests on **two moves**: (1) the prism's
[volume](/glossary.qmd#volume) is base × height, by Cavalieri-on-
slabs; (2) three congruent pyramids tile a triangular prism — so
each one is exactly *one-third*. Every other formula (oblique prism,
arbitrary pyramid, frustum) is one of these two with extra
bookkeeping.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Definitions.**

- **Prism:** a polyhedron with two parallel congruent polygonal
  bases connected by parallelograms. *Right* prism: lateral edges
  perpendicular to the base. *Regular* prism: right prism with
  regular polygonal base.
- **Pyramid:** a polyhedron with one polygonal base and an apex
  point. *Regular* pyramid: regular polygonal base + apex directly
  above the centre.
- **Cavalieri's principle:** if two solids between parallel planes
  have equal cross-section areas at every level, they have equal
  volumes.

**Theorems (Stereometry §§416–429).**

| Solid | Volume | Surface |
|-------|--------|---------|
| Rectangular box, $a \times b \times c$ | $abc$ | $2(ab + bc + ca)$ |
| Prism (right or oblique), base area $A$, height $h$ | $Ah$ | $A_{\text{lat}} + 2A$ |
| Pyramid, base area $A$, height $h$ | $\tfrac{1}{3} Ah$ | $A_{\text{lat}} + A$ |
| Regular $n$-gon prism, perimeter $P$, height $h$ | $A h$ | $Ph + 2A$ (lateral = $Ph$) |
| Regular pyramid, perim $P$, slant height $\ell$ | $\tfrac{1}{3} A h$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} P \ell + A$ |

:::

A verse:

> *Box, prism, pyramid — three solids, three formulas, one rule.*
> *Volume is base times height — that's the prism's tool.*
> *One third of that for the pyramid — three of them tile one prism.*
> *Cavalieri stitches it all: equal slices, equal volumes, no schism.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A right rectangular prism has base $4 \times 5$ and height $9$.
> Find the volume and total surface area.

$V = 4 \cdot 5 \cdot 9 = 180$ cubic units.
$S = 2(4 \cdot 5 + 5 \cdot 9 + 9 \cdot 4) = 2(20 + 45 + 36) =
2 \cdot 101 = 202$ square units.

**$V = 180,\quad S = 202.$**

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** A pyramid has square base of side $6$ and height $8$. Find
the volume.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$V = \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot 36 \cdot 8 = 96$.
</details>

**W2.** A regular hexagonal prism has base side $3$ and height $10$.
Find the volume.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Hexagon area: $\tfrac{3\sqrt 3}{2} \cdot 9 = \tfrac{27\sqrt 3}{2}$.
$V = \tfrac{27\sqrt 3}{2} \cdot 10 = 135 \sqrt 3 \approx 233.83$.
</details>

**W3.** A right square pyramid has base $4 \times 4$ and slant
height $5$. Find the lateral surface area.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Base perimeter $16$. Lateral $= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 16 \cdot 5 = 40$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A regular tetrahedron of side $s$ has all four faces
equilateral triangles. Find its volume.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use the height $h = s\sqrt{2/3}$ from Ch 1 Stretch S2. Base area
$\dfrac{\sqrt 3}{4} s^2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$V = \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt 3}{4} s^2 \cdot \dfrac{s
\sqrt 6}{3} = \dfrac{s^3 \sqrt{18}}{36} = \dfrac{s^3 \sqrt 2}{12}$.
</details>

**P2.** A pyramid has square base of side $6$ and lateral edges
all equal to $5$. Find the height.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The diagonal of the square base is $6\sqrt 2$. The lateral edge,
the half-diagonal, and the height form a right triangle.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Half-diagonal of base: $3\sqrt 2$. By Pythagoras:
$h^2 + (3\sqrt 2)^2 = 5^2 \Rightarrow h^2 = 25 - 18 = 7
\Rightarrow h = \sqrt 7$.
</details>

**P3.** Two pyramids have congruent bases and the same height, but
the apex of the second is shifted horizontally (so it's *not*
directly above the centre of the base). Show the volumes are equal.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Cavalieri.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
At every height $z$ above the base, the cross-section is similar
to the base (with linear factor $(h - z)/h$ in both pyramids). The
*shape* of the cross-section depends on apex position, but the
*area* depends only on the linear factor — hence is the same in
both pyramids. By Cavalieri, the volumes are equal. **QED.** *(The
shifted-apex pyramid has the same volume as the regular one.)*
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Show that the volume of a *frustum* (truncated pyramid with
parallel bases of areas $A_1, A_2$ and perpendicular distance $h$
between them) is
$$V = \tfrac{h}{3}(A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 A_2}).$$

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Extend the frustum into the full pyramid; subtract the small
pyramid that was cut off.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let the full pyramid have base $A_1$, height $H$. The cut-off
pyramid has similar shape with base $A_2$ and height $H - h$.
Linear scale: $\sqrt{A_2 / A_1}$. So $H - h = H \sqrt{A_2 / A_1}$,
giving $H = h / (1 - \sqrt{A_2/A_1})$. Frustum volume:
$\tfrac{1}{3} A_1 H - \tfrac{1}{3} A_2 (H - h)$. Substituting and
simplifying (multiplying through by appropriate forms of
$\sqrt{A_2/A_1}$) yields the stated formula. *(Standard frustum
volume; Stereometry §427.)*
</details>

**S2.** Show that the volumes of three pyramids on the same base
with apexes lying on the **same plane parallel to the base** are
equal — and conclude that the *centroid* of a tetrahedron divides
the line from any vertex to the centroid of the opposite face in
ratio $3:1$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
First half: same base, apexes at the same height ⇒ equal volumes
(Cavalieri).
Second half: in a tetrahedron, the four medians (vertex to
centroid of opposite face) concur at the **centroid**, dividing
each in ratio $3:1$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
First half is direct Cavalieri.

Second half: in tetrahedron $ABCD$, the median from $A$ goes to
the centroid $G_A$ of $\triangle BCD$. By a similarity-style
argument using the centroid construction (see Planimetry Stretch
S1), the four medians concur at the *3D centroid* $G$, and
$|AG| : |GG_A| = 3 : 1$. **QED.** *(Stereometry §429 — the
*tetrahedron centroid theorem*.)*
</details>

### Kiselev-style problems

Three problems in Kiselev's classical style, adapted from
Givental, *Stereometry*, §§417–429.

**K1.** Show that the volume of a *regular tetrahedron* of side $s$
is $\dfrac{s^3 \sqrt 2}{12}$, and that of a *cube* of side $s$ is
$s^3$. Compute the ratio of their volumes when both are inscribed
in the same sphere.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
A cube of side $s$ inscribes in a sphere of diameter $s\sqrt 3$
(space diagonal). A regular tetrahedron of side $s$ inscribes in
a sphere of radius $\dfrac{s \sqrt 6}{4}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
*Tetrahedron volume:* P1 above gives $\dfrac{s^3\sqrt 2}{12}$.

For the cube: side = $s$, volume $s^3$, circumsphere radius
$\dfrac{s\sqrt 3}{2}$.

Inscribed in sphere of radius $R$:
- Cube side: $s_{\text{cube}} = \dfrac{2R}{\sqrt 3}$, volume
  $\left(\dfrac{2R}{\sqrt 3}\right)^3 = \dfrac{8 R^3}{3\sqrt 3}$.
- Tetrahedron side: $s_{\text{tet}} = \dfrac{4R}{\sqrt 6}$, volume
  $\dfrac{1}{12} \sqrt 2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{4R}{\sqrt 6}\right)^3
  = \dfrac{8 R^3}{9\sqrt 3}$.

Ratio: tetrahedron / cube $= 1/3$. **The inscribed tetrahedron is
$\tfrac{1}{3}$ the volume of the inscribed cube.**
</details>

**K2.** A pyramid is *cut by a plane parallel to its base*, halfway
up. The resulting two pieces are a small pyramid (top) and a
frustum (bottom). Show the small pyramid has $\tfrac{1}{8}$ the
volume of the original.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Linear scale halves; volume scales by the cube.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The small pyramid is similar to the whole with linear ratio
$\tfrac{1}{2}$. Volume ratio: $(\tfrac{1}{2})^3 = \tfrac{1}{8}$.
**QED.** *(The frustum has $\tfrac{7}{8}$.)*
*(Stereometry §427 corollary.)*
</details>

**K3.** A regular hexagonal prism has all edges equal to $s$
(both base edges and lateral edges). Find the volume and the total
surface area.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Hexagon area: $\dfrac{3\sqrt 3}{2} s^2$. Volume: $\dfrac{3\sqrt 3}
{2} s^3$. Lateral surface: $6$ rectangles each $s \times s$, area
$6 s^2$. Two hexagonal bases: $2 \cdot \dfrac{3\sqrt 3}{2} s^2 =
3\sqrt 3 s^2$. **Total surface: $(6 + 3\sqrt 3) s^2 \approx
11.196 s^2$.** *(Stereometry §417 example.)*
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**Source pointer.** Adapted from Givental, *Kiselev's Geometry,
Book II — Stereometry*, §§416–429 (Sumizdat 2008). Free PDF on
[cimat.mx](https://www.cimat.mx/ciencia_para_jovenes/bachillerato/libros/%5BKiselev,Givental%5DKiselev's_Geom2.pdf).
The *prism-into-three-pyramids* dissection (Problem 5) is Kiselev's
§425, following Euclid XII.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this chapter, remember these:

1. A **[prism](/glossary.qmd#prism)** has two congruent parallel
   bases — its [volume](/glossary.qmd#volume) is *base area × height*,
   straight or slanted (Cavalieri).
2. A **[pyramid](/glossary.qmd#pyramid)** has one base and one apex —
   its volume is *one-third base area × height*, the 3D analogue of
   the triangle being half a parallelogram.
3. **Cavalieri's principle** — same cross-section areas at every
   level ⇒ same total volume — is the single tool that makes every
   3D volume calculation in school manageable.

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Slant height equals lateral edge."** They are different.
   The *slant height* is the perpendicular from the apex to a base
   edge (used in lateral surface). The *lateral edge* is from the
   apex to a base vertex (used in pyramid height calculations).
2. **"The pyramid volume formula needs a regular pyramid."** It does
   not. Any pyramid with base area $A$ and perpendicular height $h$
   has volume $\tfrac{1}{3} A h$, *regardless* of where the apex
   is over the base (Problem 6, P3 above).
3. **"Cavalieri requires the cross-sections to be congruent, not
   just equal in area."** Equal area is enough — that is precisely
   the strength of Cavalieri's principle (P3 demonstrates this).
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you prove the prism-volume formula (Problem 2) by base
   triangulation, on a fresh sheet?
2. Did the *prism-into-three-pyramids* dissection (Problem 5) feel
   like one move?
3. Can you explain Cavalieri's principle to a friend in two
   sentences, with an everyday example (a leaning stack of coins
   has the same volume as a straight stack)?

- **3 yes:** continue to *Cylinder and Cone* (Ch 3, the pilot).
- **2 yes:** redo Problem 5 — the dissection is the chapter's
  centrepiece.
- **0–1 yes:** Problems 4–6 form the volume-of-pyramid chain;
  re-walk them.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`kg2.lines-and-planes`](../01-lines-and-planes/index.qmd) —
  perpendicular line to a plane (defines pyramid height); plane
  positions (defines parallel cross-sections for Cavalieri).
- [`kg1.areas`](../../tier-kiselev-3-planimetry/09-areas/index.qmd) —
  every prism / pyramid volume formula bottoms out in a 2D base-
  area formula.

### What this unlocks

- [`kg2.cylinder-cone`](../03-cylinder-cone/index.qmd) — cylinders
  and cones are *limits* of regular prisms and pyramids as the
  number of base sides $n \to \infty$. The pilot uses this directly.
- **`kg2.sphere`** *(Ch 4, planned)* — Cavalieri's principle
  used to compute sphere volume against a cylinder + double-cone
  comparison.
- **`kg2.regular-polyhedra`** *(Ch 5, planned)* — the five
  Platonic solids are special pyramidal / dipyramidal
  configurations.

### Kiselev chapter alignment

- **Geometry, Book II — Chapter 2: Polyhedra (§§416–429).**
  Givental translation, Sumizdat 2008. Free PDF on
  [cimat.mx](https://www.cimat.mx/ciencia_para_jovenes/bachillerato/libros/%5BKiselev,Givental%5DKiselev's_Geom2.pdf).
- **Sister synthesis lessons:**
  [`cbse9.surface-areas-and-volumes`](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/11-surface-areas-and-volumes/index.qmd)
  (introductory mensuration) and
  [`cbse10.surface-areas-and-volumes`](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/12-surface-areas-and-volumes/index.qmd)
  (combined-solid mensuration).
- **Read alongside the source PDF** for Kiselev's full prism-into-
  three-pyramids dissection (§425).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Every 3D mesh is ultimately a **polyhedron**
— made of triangular or polygonal faces. Volume queries (for
buoyancy, mass, fluid simulation) use the same dissection-into-
prisms idea, applied to triangulated meshes via the *signed
volume of a tetrahedron from origin to triangle*.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** Cavalieri's principle is the *integration-by-slicing*
intuition that becomes Riemann's $\int A(z) dz$ formula in
multivariable calculus. The pyramid-as-one-third-of-prism is the
discrete dissection version; calculus is the continuous version.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones:** Kiselev (the §416–429 ordering); Cavalieri
  (1635); Euclid (Books XI–XII for the original prism dissections).
- **The prism-into-three-pyramids dissection (Problem 5)** is
  surprisingly subtle — Kiselev's clean version requires checking
  that *each pair* of the three pyramids has equal volume by
  Cavalieri, which is non-trivial.
- **What this lesson does not do:** prove **Hilbert's third problem**
  — that *not every two equal-volume polyhedra are scissors-
  congruent*. That is a 20th-century result (Dehn 1900) belonging
  to a measure-theory or representation-theory course.
:::
